turns.content: What's the difference between Iterator and DoubleEndedIterator?

Good question! `Iterator` is a trait that allows you to traverse elements from front-to-back with a `next()` method. On the other hand, `DoubleEndedIterator` extends this by adding a `next_back()` method, enabling traversal from back-to-front as well.
So if I implement DoubleEndedIterator, can I just call next() and then next_back()?
Yes, but implementing both is necessary. Implementing `next()` alone won't make your iterator double-ended.
How do I implement DoubleEndedIterator with next_back()?

You can implement `DoubleEndedIterator` by defining a custom struct that holds the data structure you want to iterate over. Then, in your implementation of `next_back()`, traverse from the end towards the beginning.
But what if I just want to reverse an existing iterator? Can't I use rev()?
Absolutely! `rev()` is a convenience adapter that takes any iterator and returns a new one that iterates in reverse order. It's implemented for many types, like vectors or slices.
So if I have a vector, can I do something like this?
Yes! You can call `vec.iter().rev()` to get an iterator that goes from the last element to the first.
What are some std iterators that implement DoubleEndedIterator?
`Vec<T>`, `String`, and slices all implement `DoubleEndedIterator`. You can reverse these directly with `.iter().rev()` or `.into_iter().rev()`, depending on ownership.
Can I use rev() with take() to get the last N elements?
Absolutely! Use `iter().rev().take(n)` to get the last `n` elements efficiently. This is one of the neat things about iterator adaptors.
Is there any downside to using rev() with take()?
No, but be careful if you reverse a mutable iterator. You might need to clone the data first.
